coredata.dc:title: Paper-based electrodes with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for detection of copper ions via electrochemiluminescence
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Zikang
Li, Ruirui
Li, Yipeng
Pan, Peng
Liu, Jun
Qi, Yangyang
Zhou, Baozeng
Yang, Zhengchun
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were prepared using pyrolysis, with citric acid and urea as the carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively, to be used for Cu2+ ion detection in a paper-based ECL sensor. Existing methods for Cu2+ detection require expensive equipment, specialized experimental chambers, or complex sample pretreatment procedures, which limit their functionality. Using first principles, the electronic and optical properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and N-GQDs were calculated and analyzed. The N-GQDs showed clearer green fluorescence under long-wave ultraviolet light and a better, more stable electrochemiluminescence (ECL) performance. Using first principles, the electronic and optical properties of the GQDs and N-GQDs were calculated and analyzed, and A theoretical mechanism for the observed ECL enhancement in the N-GQDs was proposed. A paper-based ECL sensor with N-GQDs was then developed, using a screen-printing technique, to detect Cu2+ ions. The results showed that the ECL intensity of the sensor increased as the Cu2+ ion concentration increased, and the sensor demonstrated a linear detection range of 0.01–1000 μM. Overall, the sensor exhibited excellent ECL stability, reproducibility, and selectivity. This simple, inexpensive, and effective monitoring technique can be used to detect Cu2+ to prevent negative effects on human health and the environment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electroluminescence
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
First principles
Cu2+ ion detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effects of N doping on the ECL intensity of N-GQDs
Electronic and optical properties of GQDs and N-GQDs
ECL mechanism of the N-GQDs sensor
Detection of copper ions using N-GQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Physical characteristics of GQDs and N-GQDs. TEM characterizations of (a) GQDs and (b) N-GQDs. Inset (top-left): PL emission under a UV lamp and (bottom-right): corresponding HRTEM images. Particle size distribution of (c) GQDs and (d) N-GQDs.

Electronic and optical properties of GQDs and N-GQDs. (a) Optimized structural model of GQDs and N-GQDs (C and N atoms are represented in grey and blue, respectively). (b) Absorption coefficients of GQDs and N-GQDs for the energy range 0–12 eV. Electronic band structure for (c) GQDs and (d) N-GQDs. Electronic DOS for (e) GQDs and (f) N-GQDs. (g) Real and (h) imaginary parts of the spectra dielectric function. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic diagram of the ECL sensor. The upper part of the figure shows the synthesis process of the N-GQDs; the lower part of the figure shows the reaction mechanism.
Optimization of the experimental conditions. Effects of different parameters on the ECL performance of N-GQDs: (a–b) scan rate; (c–d) K2S2O8 concentration; and (e–f) pH of PBS solution.

Evaluation of sensor performance. (a) ECL intensities at various Cu2+ concentrations (Z: 0.01–1000 μM). (b) ECL signal logarithmic calibration curve for different concentrations of Cu2+ (X: 0.01–1000 μM). (c) ECL selectivity towards Cu2+ detection.
Comparison of the proposed system and other published techniques for Cu2+ detection.
Determination of Cu2+ in tap water samples.

head.ce:title: Paper-based electrodes with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for detection of copper ions via electrochemiluminescence

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Integrated Circuit Science and Engineering, Advanced Materials and Printed Electronics Center, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Film Electronic & Communication Devices, Tianjin University of Technology, Tianjin, 300384, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Integrated Circuit Science and Engineering
Advanced Materials and Printed Electronics Center
Tianjin Key Laboratory of Film Electronic & Communication Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Integrated Circuit Science and Engineering, Advanced Materials and Printed Electronics Center, Tianjin Key Laboratory of Film Electronic & Communication Devices, Tianjin University of Technology, Tianjin 300384, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electrical Engineering and Automation, Tianjin University of Technology, Tianjin, 300384, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Electrical Engineering and Automation

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Electrical Engineering and Automation, Tianjin University of Technology, Tianjin 300384, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were prepared using pyrolysis, with citric acid and urea as the carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively, to be used for Cu2+ ion detection in a paper-based ECL sensor. Existing methods for Cu2+ detection require expensive equipment, specialized experimental chambers, or complex sample pretreatment procedures, which limit their functionality. Using first principles, the electronic and optical properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and N-GQDs were calculated and analyzed. The N-GQDs showed clearer green fluorescence under long-wave ultraviolet light and a better, more stable electrochemiluminescence (ECL) performance. Using first principles, the electronic and optical properties of the GQDs and N-GQDs were calculated and analyzed, and A theoretical mechanism for the observed ECL enhancement in the N-GQDs was proposed. A paper-based ECL sensor with N-GQDs was then developed, using a screen-printing technique, to detect Cu2+ ions. The results showed that the ECL intensity of the sensor increased as the Cu2+ ion concentration increased, and the sensor demonstrated a linear detection range of 0.01–1000 μM. Overall, the sensor exhibited excellent ECL stability, reproducibility, and selectivity. This simple, inexpensive, and effective monitoring technique can be used to detect Cu2+ to prevent negative effects on human health and the environment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper ions (Cu2+) are the third most abundant metal ion in the human body [1], second only to the content of iron and zinc ions. As an essential trace element, copper plays a vital role in various metabolic processes in humans and other organisms [2]. However, Cu2+ can also seriously damage animals and plants. For example, excessive Cu2+ intake can cause eczema and kidney disease in humans [3], whereas high concentrations of Cu2+ can damage DNA and chlorophyll in plants [4]. In addition, extensive use of Cu2+ can cause serious environmental problems and severely damage the self-purification ability of natural water systems [5]. The World Health Organization (WHO) and the United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) have set maximum allowable limits for copper in drinking water of 2.0 mg L−1 (30 μM) and 1.3 mg L−1 (20 μM) [6], respectively. Therefore, a fast, simple, highly sensitive, and selective technique for detecting Cu2+ is urgently needed. Some techniques have been widely reported, including spectrophotometry, fluorescence quenching [7], atomic absorption spectroscopy [8], and solid phase extraction [9]; however, these methods have requirements, such as expensive equipment, specialized experimental chambers, or complex sample pretreatment procedures, which limits their functionality. Furthermore, these methods cannot be used for real-time analysis because they cannot be miniaturized, which makes them poorly suited to widespread practical applications.

Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is a process wherein a luminescent excited state is created using free radicals that are generated by electricity from electrodes. ECL does not require an external light source, and this simplifies the requirements for the detection equipment. ECL is of great interest for sensing applications as it enables simple operation and rapid analysis while offering the advantages of high sensitivity, low background, and a wide linear range [10]. Consequently, ECL has been employed in various fields, such as drug analysis, immunoassay, and biological small-molecule analysis [11]. Thus far, different luminescent materials have been investigated for the purpose of detecting metal ions [12].

Compared with the traditional reagents used in ECL (such as luminol and ruthenium), quantum dots (QDs) have the advantages of neutral detection conditions and easy realization in cathodic and anodic processes, which make them suitable for a variety of ECL biosensing strategies [13]. In particular, GQDs, which small 0D pieces of graphene less than 20 nm in diameter [14], are expected to be widely used in applications in biotechnology and the environmental sciences owing to their significant potential as non-toxic, fluorescent alternatives to conventional semiconductor quantum dots [15]. Based on previous research, heteroatom-doped GQDs, particularly nitrogen-doped, could be effectively tuned for their natural properties, including electronic characteristics, surface features, and local chemical properties [16]. Graphene's unparalleled electronic attributes, for instance, have been thoroughly characterized and elucidated [17]; these include outstanding intrinsic charge carrier mobility, a near-zero band gap energy, the capacity to sustain current densities over one million times greater than those tolerated by conductors such as copper, and a single-sheet resistivity of only 10−6 Ω cm2 [18]. Li et al. prepared nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots using glucosamine as the carbon and nitrogen sources [19]; the quantum dots showed good performance. However, glucosamine has only one N atom, whereas urea has two N atoms; therefore, we used urea as the nitrogen source and improved the preparation method.

Furthermore, paper-based analytical devices provide end-users with portable analytical platforms for applications in different research areas owing to their low cost and compatibility, as well as their ease of detection, use, and handling [20]. These devices use paper chips that are fabricated using a screen-printing technique with pinealol as the hydrophobic agent. By changing the design of the hydrophilic and hydrophobic regions on the paper chip, different fluidic channels can be used to accomplish different tasks. Moreover, the ink preparation is simple, the system is easy to operate, and large numbers of chips can be printed.

This paper presents a facile and efficient method for synthesizing, by direct heating, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs), with carbon and nitrogen sources from citric acid and urea, respectively. The obtained N-GQDs were characterized and simulated to explore their structural and electronic properties. When used in combination with the co-reactant K2S2O8, the N-GQDs perform Cu2+ ion detection in paper-based ECL sensors.

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were measured with a METASH UV-5100B spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) absorption spectra were acquired using a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer. The elemental components of the GQDs were investigated using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Thermo Scientific, ESCALAB 250Xi). The microscopic morphology of the GQDs was examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM; FEI, Talos F200X). The ECL intensities and cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of the GQDs and N-GQDs were obtained using an MPI-EII workstation (MPI-EII, Xi'an Remax Electronic Science & Technology Co., Ltd., China).

Citric acid, urea, potassium persulfate (K2S2O8), potassium chloride, copper chloride, sodium chloride, nickel chloride, iron chloride, manganese chloride, zinc chloride, aluminum chloride, ethyl cellulose, pine oleyl alcohol, oily pigments, cellulose paper, phosphate-buffered saline solution (PBS), carbon paste, and silver paste were purchased from Tianjin Jiangtian Technology Co., Ltd., China. The experimental reagents used were of analytical grade and were not purified further. PBS was prepared and diluted with deionized water (DI) as the solvent.

The N-GQDs were prepared by direct pyrolysis using a method described by Gu et al. [21] with some modifications, as follows. First, 2 g of citric acid and 2 g of urea were placed into a 50 mL crucible and heated to 200 °C [22] (Fig. 3). After approximately 5 min, the citric acid and urea were dissolved. Subsequently, within 15 min, the solution changed from colorless to pale yellow to a brownish-yellow liquid. Then, 10 g L−1 of NaOH liquid was added dropwise with a plastic-tipped dropper, and the mixture was stirred. After it was neutralized to pH 7 using NaOH, the aqueous solution for the N-GQDs was obtained. A similar method was used to prepare the solution for the GQDs, except that urea was not added.

First-principle computational simulations and analyses of two classes of quantum dots were performed using the density generalized function of the Vienna atomic simulation package. Projected augmented waves were used to describe the electron–ion interaction system, using the Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof generalized gradient approximation exchange correlation function. The cutoff energy of the wave function expansion was set to 400 eV. The model consisted of a 5 × 5 supercell with a vacuum layer of 15 Å in the Z direction to eliminate adjacent interlayer interactions. To optimize the geometry, a 5 × 5 × 1 Monkhorst–Pack grid was used with ion relaxation energies of less than 0.05 eV and electron relaxation energies of less than 0.00001 eV. When the electronic density of states (DOS) was calculated, a denser network of K-points (9 × 9 × 1) was employed. All calculations were performed in the non-spin-polarized mode.

The CV and ECL tests used paper-based electrodes, and all the experiments were performed at room temperature (∼25 °C). The paper-based electrodes were prepared according to protocols for a study previously published by our laboratory [19]. Next, 20 μL of aqueous N-GQD solution was added dropwise to the upper surface of the paper working electrode, which was then dried, washed with DI, and set aside. The N-GQD paper chips were then immobilized and attached to the device for ECL analysis. The influence of pH level on ECL was investigated in 10 mM PBS (pH 4–10). A solution of 100 mM potassium chloride (as the electrolyte) and 100 mM of K2S2O8 (as the co-reactant) was prepared. Subsequently, 40 μL of Cu2+ ion PBS solutions with different concentrations were added dropwise onto the modified paper working electrodes. CV was used to observe the ECL at scan potentials between −2 and −0.5 V and a scan rate of 100 mV−1. During ECL detection, the voltage of the photomultiplier tube was set to 900 V. The limit of detection (LOD) for Cu2+ was calculated as
L O D = 3
S b
/ m
, where
m
is the slope of the corresponding calibration curve and
S b
is the standard deviation for the blank responses [23].

As shown in Fig. 1 (a and b), the size distributions of the GQDs and N-GQDs are relatively uniform. The insets in the bottom–right corners of parts a and b of Fig. 1 show the particle fluorescences under UV light, which are blue and green, respectively, with the N-GQDs producing a brighter emission. Fig. 1(c and d) shows high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images with well-resolved lattice fringes and a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which is related to the (100) plane of graphene [24]. Fig. 1(e and f) shows the particle size distribution in the 10 nm size image, including the average sizes of the two types of nanoparticles: approximately 2.93 nm for the GQDs and approximately 2.46 nm for the N-GQDs. The distribution of the N-GQDs was narrower than that of the GQDs; that is, the particles were more uniform in size, and they were also smaller.

To explore the mechanism underlying the ECL signal enhancement of NGQDs further, we investigated the UV–Vis absorption and PL emission spectra. The UV–Vis spectra of aqueous solutions of GQDs (Fig. S1(a)) and N-GQDs (Fig. S1(b)) revealed absorption peaks at approximately 206 and 340 nm, respectively. In general, the absorption band at 206 nm is attributed to the
π →
π *
leap of CC in the sp2 C domain, whereas the absorption peak at 340 nm is conferred by the
n →
π *
leap of CO [25]. Parts c and d of Fig. S1 show the variations in the PL emission as the excitation wavelength increases from 320 to 370 nm for the GQDs and NGQDs, respectively. The PL emission peak of the GQDs is red-shifted from 420 to 445 nm and that of N-GQDs is red-shifted from 440 to 455 nm. The red-shift phenomenon weakens as the excitation wavelength increases, which may be due to the formation of oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of the N-GQDs [26].

The elemental composition of the as-prepared N-GQDs was investigated using the XPS spectra, as shown in Fig. S2. The full XPS spectra exhibit three main peak signals at 284.8, 399.9, and 531.7 eV, which correspond to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively (Fig. S2(a)). The C1s spectrum consists of four fitted peaks at 284.6, 285.7, 286.8, and 288.3 eV, which correspond to the C–C, C–N, C–O, and CO bonds, respectively (Fig. S2(b)). The binding energy of the N1s peak is 399.9 eV (Fig. S2(c)), indicating that nitrogen mostly exists in the form of pyrrole nitrogen [27]. Finally, the O1s spectral fitting peaks at 531.5 and 533.9 eV correspond to the CO and C–O bonds, respectively (Fig. S2(d)). The characterization data indicates that N was successfully doped into the N-GQDs. Moreover, the as-prepared N-GQDs are rich in hydrophilic groups and have better surface passivation and carbonization than the GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of N doping on the ECL intensity of N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In the control experiments, the nitrogen doping variable was used as the reference material. The GQDs and N-GQDs were prepared in the same way using citric acid, and citric acid with urea, respectively. Parts a and b of Fig. S3 show a comparison of the CV and ECL intensities measured by the GQD and N-GQD modified paper-based working electrodes, respectively, in the PBS buffer solution (with 100 mM of KCl and 100 mM of K2S2O8). The peak potential of the CV curves of the GQDs and N-GQDs was approximately −1.99 V. In comparison, the peak current of the former was approximately −4.4 mA (Fig. S3(a)) and that of the latter was approximately −6.9 mA, which indicated that the N-GQDs were the most likely to obtain electrons. This is because N doping increases the surface traps of the N-GQDs, which facilitates the electron transfer. This means that the N-GQDs generate more free radicals, and thus exhibit stronger and more stable ECL signals. The band structures in Fig. 2 (c and d) also illustrate that the doping by N atoms enlarges the graphene band gap and enhances the electron transfer.

In addition, to evaluate the repeatability of the ECL sensors, we prepared six paper-based/N-GQDs sensors in the same experimental environment and analyzed the experimental results of six sets of consecutive scans under the same test conditions (Fig. S4), yielding a relative standard deviation (n = 6) of 1.5%. These experiments demonstrated the good repeatability and stability of the ECL sensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electronic and optical properties of GQDs and N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, N was used as a doping element for the GQDs, the influence of N doping was investigated, and first-principle calculations for the N-GQDs were performed. According to the XPS spectra, the ratio of C and N atoms was approximately 5.01%, and this was used to simulate optimized structures for the GQDs and N-GQDs (Fig. 2(a)). Using random substitution, five possible models were proposed for the structure of the N-GQDs. Nitrogen doping increases the band gap of graphene and, as shown in Table S1, the structure in the first row had the largest band gap; therefore, it was selected as the ideal model for further analysis. The relevant electronic and optical properties were analyzed as follows.

The band structure is related to the electronic energy levels of the crystal structure, and it is crucial for elucidating many fundamental properties of the crystal. According to the band structure analysis (Fig. 2(c and d)), intrinsic graphene is semi-metallic in nature and has a zero band gap, which agrees with results reported elsewhere [28]. Moreover, the N doping leads to a shift of the Fermi energy level to the conduction band, which indicates that n-type doping occurs. The above results agree with the findings of Wu et al. [29] and Kim et al. [30]. Thus, N doping introduces a donor state, which enhances electron transfer and increases external conductivity. Moreover, N doping produces a direct band gap at the Dirac point of approximately 0.81 eV, owing to the destruction of the graphene lattice symmetry by the doping process.

In electron doping, more electron states are pulled below the Fermi level. Therefore, doping with foreign atoms can be conducted to open the graphene band gap, and this affects its optical properties. The total DOS of both structures (Fig. 2(e and f)) were calculated, as were the split-orbit DOS of the C and N atoms in the N-GQDs. N doping caused the Fermi level to shift by 1.1 eV to the valence band and introduced an energy gap. This indicates that N-doped graphene can become an n-type semiconductor. Fig. S5 shows that, after N doping, the impurity band generated near the Fermi level of the total DOS is a result of the contribution from the n–p orbitals. N doping introduces electrons into graphene; therefore, when N-doped graphene interacts with the electrodes, a large charge can be transferred to the graphene.

The dielectric function is an important optical parameter composed of real and imaginary parts. As shown in Fig. 2(g, h), N doping decreases the photon energy corresponding to the onset of the imaginary part, indicating that electrons are more likely to transition. The absorption coefficient is a measure of the distance that a beam with a certain energy will penetrate into a material before it is absorbed. Fig. 2(b) shows the spectral distribution of the absorption coefficients. When N atoms replace C atoms, the material undergoes a red shift. Concurrently, the absorption peak becomes broader and the absorption intensity decreases. This suggests that N doping improves the electro-optic properties of the materials and the material's response to visible light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL mechanism of the N-GQDs sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
The ECL mechanism of the sensor with the N-GQDs is shown in Fig. 3 . The ECL produced by the N-GQDs and K2S2O8 originates from the fundamental principle of ECL from the co-reactant. Firstly, K2S2O8 acts as a co-reactant to gain electrons and is reduced to SO4 2− and SO4 •–. Simultaneously, the N-GQDs are reduced to the state of the anionic N-GQD•– radicals. The SO4 •– radicals, which have strong oxidizing properties, undergo an electron conversion annihilation reaction with the N-GQD•– radicals to generate excited-state N-GQDs*. Finally, the excited state N-GQDs* release energy and emit light as they return to the ground state [31]. This mechanistic process can be summarized by the following reactions.
(1)
S2O8 2− + e− → SO4 2− + SO4 •−;
(2)
N-GQD + e− →N-GQD•−;
(3)
N-GQD•− + SO4 •− →N-GQD* + SO4 2−;
(4)
N-GQD* →N-GQD + h
υ
.

We selected the N-GQDs for the subsequent experimental studies. The scan rate, concentration of K2S2O8, and pH of the PBS solution were the variables in the CV and ECL experimental tests. It was necessary to optimize these factors to ensure an optimal solution environment for Cu2+ detection.

Fig. 4 (a and b) shows that the ECL intensity increased at first, then decreased, for scan rates of 0.05–0.2 V s−1. This finding indicates that the most suitable scan rate was 0.1 V s−1. Next, the ECL intensities at different K2S2O8 concentrations showed that 0.15 M could be considered to be the optimal concentration of K2S2O8 (Fig. 4(c and d)). Fig. 4(e and f) shows the influence of the pH of the PBS solution on the ECL intensity produced by the N-GQDs. The ECL intensity increases and then decreases with the increasing pH, and the strongest ECL intensity is observed at pH 7, which is the optimal pH. And we use the optimal conditions, after 10, 20 and 30 days, the paper-based sensor still shows good ECL stability (Fig. S6).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of copper ions using N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: CuCl2 (100 μM) was chosen as the substance to be examined and was placed into the N-GQDs/K2S2O8 system, causing the peak currents in the CV and ECL intensity to exhibit significant increases (Fig. S7), demonstrating that Cu2+ has a facilitative effect on the reaction of the paper-based ECL system. Fig. S6 shows that, as the potential swept from −0.5 to −2.0 V, the ECL onset potential was approximately −1.2 V when Cu2+ was not added, which is higher than the corresponding value of −1.05 V when Cu2+ was added. The higher peak current in the N-GQDs/K2S2O8–Cu2+ system and decreasing potential with increasing ECL intensity demonstrate that Cu2+ can accelerate electron transfer between the electrodes in solution and the N-GQDs/K2S2O8 system, thereby enhancing the ECL intensity of the sensor [32]. This phenomenon is likely caused by the catalysis of the aromatic ring compounds by the CuCl2 in aqueous PBS [33]. Moreover, an electronic bridge channel is built by the metal and free radical coordination between the N-GQDs and K2S2O8. This characteristic accelerates and directs the directional transfer of electrons and free radicals, facilitating the smooth progress of the reduction reaction, and thus increasing the ECL signal.

Fig. 5 shows the sensor being used to detect various copper ion concentrations. Under optimal conditions, the ECL intensity gradually increases as the copper ion concentration increases, owing to the promotional influence of Cu2+ (Fig. 5(a)). A strong linear relationship between the logarithmic concentrations of Cu2+ was obtained for the range 0.01–1000 μM (Fig. 5(b)). These results show that the synthesized N-GQDs–ECL system can detect Cu2+ in a certain concentration range and that Cu2+ enhances the ECL system.

Sensor performance can be evaluated with selectivity as a necessary factor. Therefore, equal concentrations (100 μM) of different metal chlorides were tested against the background of identical optimal experimental conditions. Fig. 5(c) shows that the addition of other metal ions had little effect on the ECL intensity, and only Cu2+ significantly increased the ECL intensity. This indicates that the sensor was highly selective for the detection of Cu2+. Furthermore, comparison with previous reports shows that the proposed design outperforms most existing sensors in terms of Cu2+ detection (Table 1 ).

The method was applied to the determination of Cu2+ in tap water samples to assess the applicability of the method to real samples. First, Cu2+ concentrations of 10, 50, and 100 μM were selected and added to tap water, and then applied to paper-based/NGQDs sensors for ECL testing. The results obtained were subsequently compared with the test values from UV–Vis spectrometry and showed consistent results. The recovery results are shown in Table 2 of the supplementary material and demonstrate good performance, which indicates the stability of the sensor system for potential use in the detection of Cu2+ in real samples.

A facile method of synthesizing N-GQDs was developed, and successful N doping was demonstrated through a series of characterizations. Nitrogen doping was shown to improve the performance and stability of the ECL reaction. The band structures, DOS, dielectric functions, and absorption coefficients of GQDs and N-GQDs were theoretically calculated. The results demonstrated that N-GQDs exhibit better electro-optic properties than the undoped GQDs, which explains the mechanism of ECL enhancement. Furthermore, the ECL sensor exhibited linear sensitivity (Fig. 5(b)) to Cu2+ ions and good selectivity. These findings will help promote the exploration of the synthesis and theoretical calculation of N-GQDs and their application to disposable paper-based ECL sensors for Cu2+ ion detection.

Zikang Zhu: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Ruirui Li: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Yipeng Li: Investigation, Data curation. Peng Pan: Conceptualization, Supervision, Methodology. Jun Liu: Data curation. Yangyang Qi: Supervision. Baozeng Zhou: Data curation. Zhengchun Yang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the support by Tianjin Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. 18JCZDJC99800), National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51502203), Tianjin Distinguished Professor Foundation of Young Researcher, and Tianjin Development Program for Innovation and Entrepreneurship.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matchemphys.2023.127300.

J. Toxicol. Environ. Health B Crit. Rev.

Starr, Copper and human health: biochemistry, genetics, and strategies for modeling dose-response relationships, J Toxicol Environ Health B Crit Rev, 10 (2007) 157-222.
